title: Treehouse Of Horror

jokes.text: Hello, everyone. You know, Halloween is a strange holiday. I don't understand it. Kids worshiping ghosts, pretending to be devils... things on TV that are inappropriate for children.
jokes.setup: Marge introduces herself as a concerned parent warning about inappropriate TV content
jokes.punchline: The irony that she's introducing exactly the kind of inappropriate TV show she's warning against
jokes.explanation: Classic Simpsons meta-humor with Marge's character-appropriate moral panic being used to lampoon TV content warnings while simultaneously delivering one

jokes.text: But tonight's show, which I wash my hands of, is really scary.
jokes.setup: Marge continues her warning about inappropriate content
jokes.punchline: The phrase 'wash my hands of' creates comic distance while she's literally introducing the show
jokes.explanation: Good character work with Marge trying to have it both ways - introducing scary content while disclaiming responsibility

jokes.text: ...instead of writing us angry letters. Thanks for your attention.
jokes.setup: Marge suggests parents put sensitive kids to bed
jokes.punchline: The direct acknowledgment of viewer complaints and the polite 'thanks for your attention' after basically telling parents to deal with it
jokes.explanation: Decent meta-humor about network TV and viewer complaints, though somewhat predictable

jokes.text: But it was too late. End of story. I heard that in the third grade. It's not scary.
jokes.setup: Lisa tells a classic scary story
jokes.punchline: Bart's dismissive 'I heard that in the third grade' deflates the scary story setup
jokes.explanation: Typical sibling dynamic with Bart's world-weary dismissal of Lisa's attempt at scary storytelling

jokes.text: -Here's a story that's scarifying. -Oh, brother.
jokes.setup: Lisa wants to tell a scarier story than Bart's dismissal suggests she can
jokes.punchline: The made-up word 'scarifying' and Bart's immediate eye-roll response
jokes.explanation: Lisa's nerdy attempt to coin a scary word gets the predictable Bart reaction - good character interplay

jokes.text: -I'm glad there's a curse here. -Huh?
jokes.setup: Normal house-buying conversation
jokes.punchline: Homer casually mentioning he's glad about a curse while the agent is confused
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - finding the positive in supernatural danger, delivered with characteristic obliviousness

jokes.text: Well, he must have been motivated. Prime location, 18 rooms, moat. We shouldn't be able to afford this.
jokes.setup: Marge lists the house's impressive features
jokes.punchline: The casual mention of a 'moat' among normal house features
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist detail - moat treated as just another house feature alongside normal amenities

jokes.text: -What on earth was that? -Oh, just the house settling.
jokes.setup: Mysterious supernatural voice tells them to 'Get out'
jokes.punchline: Homer's completely casual dismissal as normal 'house settling'
jokes.explanation: Classic horror movie trope subversion with Homer's oblivious rationalization of obvious supernatural phenomena

jokes.text: Looks like a vortex. A gateway into another dimension.
jokes.setup: Marge asks about something strange in the corner
jokes.punchline: Homer's matter-of-fact identification of an interdimensional portal
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character comedy - Homer casually identifying something impossible with the same tone he'd use for identifying a stain

jokes.text: Ooh, a vortex. Catch!
jokes.setup: Homer identifies an interdimensional vortex
jokes.punchline: He immediately starts playing catch with it, throwing garbage through
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - finding the most mundane use for something supernatural. The childlike 'Catch!' is perfectly in character

jokes.text: Quit throwing garbage into our dimension.
jokes.setup: Homer throws garbage into the vortex
jokes.punchline: The other dimension complains about littering
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist humor - interdimensional beings reduced to complaining about littering like annoyed neighbors. Extremely quotable.

jokes.text: It's a fixer-upper. What's the problem?
jokes.setup: Marge wants to leave the evil house
jokes.punchline: Homer treating supernatural evil as just normal home maintenance issues
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy with Homer's practical approach to the supernatural - evil presence is just another home improvement project

jokes.text: We're not talking about a few dollars. We're talking a few thousand dollars!
jokes.setup: Marge doesn't want to live in an evil house to save money
jokes.punchline: Homer's escalation reveals the stakes are higher than initially implied
jokes.explanation: Decent character moment showing Homer's priorities - money over family safety - though fairly predictable

jokes.text: It's got great high ceilings!
jokes.setup: Homer is trying to convince Marge to stay in the evil house
jokes.punchline: He pivots to highlighting mundane real estate features while evil voices echo around them
jokes.explanation: Good juxtaposition of Homer's real estate salesman pitch against the supernatural horror backdrop

jokes.text: -Are you my conscience? -I'm-- Yes, I am.
jokes.setup: Evil house is trying to corrupt Bart
jokes.punchline: The house awkwardly agrees to be his conscience when asked directly
jokes.explanation: Funny subversion - ancient evil reduced to being polite when directly questioned, the hesitation makes it funnier

jokes.text: -Sorry, Dad. -Sorry, Bart. -Sorry, Mom.
jokes.setup: Family was in supernatural knife fight
jokes.punchline: They all politely apologize to each other like after a normal family squabble
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist humor - treating supernatural violence like everyday family bickering with polite apologies

jokes.text: This family has had its differences... but we've never had knife fights. I blame this house.
jokes.setup: Marge explaining why they need to leave
jokes.punchline: The matter-of-fact acknowledgment that knife fights are where they draw the line
jokes.explanation: Perfect Marge logic - acknowledging family dysfunction but drawing reasonable boundaries at attempted murder

jokes.text: -It's an ancient Indian burial ground. -Man, this place has everything!
jokes.setup: Lisa discovers the classic horror movie explanation for the evil house
jokes.punchline: Homer treats it as another selling point rather than a terrifying revelation
jokes.explanation: Excellent subversion of horror tropes with Homer's enthusiastic real estate mindset - burial ground as amenity rather than curse

jokes.text: He says he mentioned it.
jokes.setup: Homer just had heated phone argument with real estate agent
jokes.punchline: His casual acceptance and reporting of the agent's claim
jokes.explanation: Decent follow-up showing Homer's quick surrender and how easily he's manipulated by authority figures

jokes.text: Stop saying those horrible things and show some manners!
jokes.setup: Evil house is threatening them with graphic violence
jokes.punchline: Marge treating supernatural evil like a rude dinner guest
jokes.explanation: Perfect Marge moment - applying maternal authority to supernatural horror. The politeness expectation for ancient evil is brilliantly absurd

jokes.text: Come on! Do it! Do it! Do it!
jokes.setup: House reluctantly makes walls bleed on command
jokes.punchline: Homer getting excited and demanding more supernatural entertainment like a kid at a magic show
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy with Homer's childlike enthusiasm turning supernatural horror into entertainment

jokes.text: Why are you scaring us? To keep us from getting close... and maybe even loving you?
jokes.setup: House is trying to frighten them away
jokes.punchline: Marge psychoanalyzing the evil house like it has abandonment issues
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Marge moment - treating supernatural evil like an emotionally damaged family member who needs nurturing

jokes.text: My name is Marge Simpson, and we're not going anywhere. We're going to have to live together, so you better get used to it. Please.
jokes.setup: Marge is addressing the evil house
jokes.punchline: She uses her maternal authority to essentially ground an ancient evil, ending with polite 'please'
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation of Marge's nurturing approach - introducing herself formally to evil and setting household rules with characteristic politeness

jokes.text: Life with the Simpsons. What choice do I have?
jokes.setup: Ancient evil house contemplating living with the family
jokes.punchline: The house's resigned acceptance that the Simpsons are the real horror
jokes.explanation: Brilliant reversal - the supernatural evil realizes the Simpsons are worse than any ancient curse. The defeated tone is perfect

jokes.text: It destroyed itself rather than live with us. You can't help but feel a little rejected.
jokes.setup: Evil house destroys itself to escape the Simpsons
jokes.punchline: Homer takes supernatural suicide as personal rejection rather than victory
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes - perfect character comedy with Homer's self-centered interpretation of supernatural horror as personal slight. Extremely quotable.

jokes.text: Eww! Baby spit!
jokes.setup: Lisa shows Bart a severed finger
jokes.punchline: Bart is more grossed out by baby saliva than a severed body part
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Bart's twisted priorities - supernatural horror is fine but baby spit is disgusting

jokes.text: Ooh! That was a big mama.
jokes.setup: Homer uses bug zapper
jokes.punchline: His satisfied comment about killing a large bug
jokes.explanation: Minor character moment - Homer's simple satisfaction with bug zapping, fairly predictable blue-collar humor

jokes.text: Cool, man!
jokes.setup: UFO appears and abducts the family
jokes.punchline: Bart's casual, positive reaction to alien abduction
jokes.explanation: Good Bart character moment - treating alien abduction as exciting rather than terrifying, typical kid reaction

jokes.text: -You speak English. -I'm speaking Rigelian. By coincidence, our languages are exactly the same.
jokes.setup: Homer notices the aliens speak English
jokes.punchline: The absurd explanation that two completely different planets evolved identical languages by coincidence
jokes.explanation: Excellent meta-humor addressing the universal sci-fi trope of aliens speaking perfect English with a brilliantly absurd explanation

jokes.text: To pronounce it correctly, I would have to pull out your tongue.
jokes.setup: Homer asks for the alien's name
jokes.punchline: The casual mention of tongue removal as pronunciation requirement
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor with the matter-of-fact delivery of violent requirement for alien pronunciation

jokes.text: These are hard to make. They're an advanced race.
jokes.setup: Marge notices radish rosettes among the alien feast
jokes.punchline: She judges alien civilization advancement by their food preparation skills
jokes.explanation: Perfect Marge logic - using domestic skills as the measure of cosmic civilization advancement, very character-appropriate

jokes.text: The girl's right. Let's get some applesauce for these pork chops!
jokes.setup: Lisa expresses suspicion about the aliens' motives
jokes.punchline: Homer completely misinterprets her concern as a menu suggestion
jokes.explanation: Good Homer character moment - his food obsession preventing him from processing obvious danger warnings

jokes.text: -What are you looking at, buddy? -Your wife's quite a dish.
jokes.setup: Homer notices alien staring at Marge
jokes.punchline: The double meaning of 'dish' - both compliment and literal food reference given the cannibalistic plot
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay that works on two levels - innocent compliment to Homer but ominous food reference for the audience

jokes.text: -Do you get HBO? -No. That's extra.
jokes.setup: Aliens boast about getting millions of galactic TV channels
jokes.punchline: Even advanced aliens are limited by premium cable pricing
jokes.explanation: Excellent observational humor about cable TV economics applied to cosmic scale - universal corporate greed

jokes.text: -That's just Pong! -Get with the times, man.
jokes.setup: Aliens proudly display their 'advanced' electronic table tennis
jokes.punchline: Bart recognizes it as the primitive video game Pong, Homer tells him to be polite
jokes.explanation: Good tech humor about advanced aliens having primitive games, Homer's politeness adds character touch

jokes.text: Anyone that has mastered intergalactic travel, raise your hand. All right, then.
jokes.setup: Homer and family criticize alien technology
jokes.punchline: Alien makes logical point about their superior space travel abilities, Homer concedes immediately
jokes.explanation: Decent logical humor with alien making fair point about technological superiority, Homer's quick surrender is in character

jokes.text: I have a feeling you'll be the guests of honor.
jokes.setup: Family asks if they'll be invited to the feast on Rigel 4
jokes.punchline: The ominous double meaning of being 'guests of honor' at their own consumption
jokes.explanation: Standard dark humor setup for the cannibalism plot - works but fairly predictable horror trope

jokes.text: When we arrive, there will be plenty of time to chew the fat.
jokes.setup: Family wants to know more about the feast
jokes.punchline: Double meaning of 'chew the fat' as both casual conversation and literal cannibalism
jokes.explanation: Solid wordplay that works on two levels - innocent idiom with sinister literal meaning for the cannibalism plot

jokes.text: It's a harmless cookbook. It's just a little dusty.
jokes.setup: Family discovers alien cookbook and assumes it's for eating humans
jokes.punchline: Alien casually dismisses their fear while cleaning dust off the 'To Serve Man' cookbook
jokes.explanation: References classic Twilight Zone episode but execution is straightforward - the misdirection works but isn't particularly original

jokes.text: Let me get this straight. They thought we would eat them.
jokes.setup: Dust reveals cookbook is actually 'How to Cook for Forty Humans'
jokes.punchline: Alien's genuine confusion and hurt that humans thought they were cannibals
jokes.explanation: Good reversal of expectations - the aliens' genuine hurt feelings at being misunderstood creates sympathy for former villains

jokes.text: Wait, there's still more space dust on here.
jokes.setup: Previous dust excuse for 'How To Cook Humans' book
jokes.punchline: More dust reveals title progression, building to full reveal
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation of Twilight Zone parody. The building suspense through dust removal is expertly crafted.

jokes.text: Well, if you wanted to make Serak cry, mission accomplished.
jokes.setup: Aliens are hurt by humans' suspicion
jokes.punchline: The dramatic, wounded response with the alien name 'Serak' being unexpectedly emotional
jokes.explanation: Good character moment making the aliens unexpectedly vulnerable and emotional - humanizing the cosmic beings

jokes.text: For a superior race, they really rub it in.
jokes.setup: Aliens give elaborate speech about the paradise humans have lost through mistrust
jokes.punchline: Homer's petty complaint about aliens being sore winners
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer response - missing the cosmic significance and focusing on the aliens' attitude problem instead

jokes.text: There were monsters on that ship, and we were them.
jokes.setup: Family reflects on their experience with the benevolent aliens
jokes.punchline: Lisa's self-aware realization that humans were the real monsters through their mistrust
jokes.explanation: Good moral insight from Lisa that ties up the story theme, though somewhat predictable 'humans are the real monsters' message

jokes.text: -Way to go, Lis. -Yeah, thanks, Lisa.
jokes.setup: Lisa realizes they ruined their chance at paradise through suspicion
jokes.punchline: Family sarcastically thanks Lisa for her smart thinking that cost them eternal happiness
jokes.explanation: Decent family dynamics with typical blame-shifting after Lisa's intelligence causes problems for everyone

jokes.text: -Wait. That's a schoolbook. -Don't worry, you won't learn anything.
jokes.setup: Bart objects to Lisa reading from a schoolbook on Halloween
jokes.punchline: Lisa's reassurance that education won't interfere with entertainment
jokes.explanation: Smart meta-joke about educational content in entertainment - Lisa acknowledging the anti-learning bias while being educational

jokes.text: -Are we scared yet? -Bart, he's establishing mood.
jokes.setup: Bart gets impatient during the atmospheric opening of The Raven
jokes.punchline: Lisa defending literary technique to her impatient brother
jokes.explanation: Good character contrast between Bart's need for immediate gratification and Lisa's appreciation for artistic craft

jokes.text: This better be good.
jokes.setup: Homer opens the chamber door in the Poe adaptation expecting something scary
jokes.punchline: His practical, consumer-like demand for quality entertainment from cosmic darkness
jokes.explanation: Good Homer moment treating supernatural mystery like a disappointing TV show - consumer mentality applied to cosmic horror

jokes.text: You know what's scarier? Anything!
jokes.setup: Bart is unimpressed with the atmospheric poetry
jokes.punchline: His sweeping dismissal that literally anything would be scarier than Poe
jokes.explanation: Decent Bart line showing his impatience with classic literature - the hyperbole is appropriately childish

jokes.text: -Eat my shorts! -He says, 'Nevermore.' And that's all he'll ever say.
jokes.setup: Lisa explains the Raven will only say 'Nevermore'
jokes.punchline: Bart-as-Raven immediately breaks character with his signature catchphrase
jokes.explanation: Excellent character integration - Bart can't help being Bart even when playing a literary character. Perfect callback to his catchphrase.

jokes.text: -Perfumed by some unseen censer -Stupid censer.
jokes.setup: Homer reciting Poe's elaborate atmospheric description
jokes.punchline: He breaks character to insult the poetic imagery
jokes.explanation: Good Homer moment showing his inability to appreciate high culture - even while reciting it, he has to criticize it

jokes.text: -Nevermore -Why you little--
jokes.setup: Raven continues saying 'Nevermore' as in the original poem
jokes.punchline: Homer breaks character to strangle Bart, his signature frustrated parent response
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Homer's parental frustration overrides literary performance, classic callback to his signature move

jokes.text: It was written in 1845. Maybe people were easier to scare.
jokes.setup: Bart complains The Raven wasn't scary
jokes.punchline: Lisa's thoughtful historical perspective on changing entertainment standards
jokes.explanation: Smart Lisa observation about historical context and desensitization - shows her intellectual approach to cultural criticism

jokes.text: Like Friday the 13th Part I. It's pretty tame by today's standards.
jokes.setup: Lisa suggests people were easier to scare in 1845
jokes.punchline: Bart applies the same logic to horror movie evolution
jokes.explanation: Decent follow-up showing Bart understands Lisa's point about desensitization, though the reference is somewhat obvious

jokes.text: They're just children's stories. They can't hurt you.
jokes.setup: Marge wants to sleep with lights on after the scary stories
jokes.punchline: Homer's rational dismissal immediately before he's presumably scared by something
jokes.explanation: Standard horror story ending setup - the skeptic's dismissal followed by comeuppance, fairly predictable

tmdb_overview: The Simpson kids hide in the family treehouse and tell each other spooky stories. The first regards haunted houses, the second introduces Kang and Kodos for the first time in an alien abduction, and the third is a Simpson-adapted retelling of The Raven.

guest_stars.name: James Earl Jones
guest_stars.character: Moving Man / Serak the Preparer / Narrator (voice)

insight: Halloween special sustains 3.4 jokes per minute across three horror parodies.
